claim: Hey Blake, how are you applying all this at Boom Supersonic?
speaker: Nivi

claim: It completely changes the role of software and hardware developers.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: From day one we tried to take a lot of traditional engineering workflows—hardware engineering workflows—and turn them into software.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: If you haven’t been around hardware engineering, let me try to make this clear.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: A lot of hardware engineering happens in Excel spreadsheets on engineers’ laptops in a silo.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: Very complex spreadsheets, sometimes with VBScript code.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: All of this is actually software, but it’s treated as if it’s not.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: There’s no source control, no automated testing.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: If you want to hand something off from an aerodynamicist to a structures engineer, that’s done manually with a spreadsheet over email.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: So we started building software frameworks to automate and make repeatable hardware engineering flows, with the idea that we could reduce the cost of iteration.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: But it was slow going—we could never afford enough software engineers.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: What we’ve gotten into now is a mind-blowingly different model: the software engineers create the architectures, because they understand systems, algorithms, and division of concerns.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: Then the hardware engineers can vibe-code their pieces because they know hardware engineering.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: The result is mind-blowingly different productivity for small teams.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: If you’re designing a turbine blade—classically, a turbine blade starts cold, but when it runs it gets hot, so it gets bigger.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: You have to design both the aerodynamics and the structural design to work in its cold shape and its hot shape.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: You have to convert between cold and hot, between structures and aerodynamics.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: This takes one engineer one day for one blade for one piece of the analysis.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: There are about a thousand blades in a jet engine.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: Now, with a combination of software and hardware people creating the solution, you can change blade geometry and see in real time the structures and aerodynamics results.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: Two engineers can design an entire jet engine.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: One of the things you mentioned is that software engineers are creating the tools and architectures for the rest of the engineers.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: To me, that’s the biggest cataclysm of enterprise software—there’s no startup that builds hardware collaboration tools that can sell you anything anymore.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Internally, you’re just coding the right thing you need at any given time.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: The reason spreadsheets were successful is that no one could build custom software.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: The thing that approximates custom software the most is a spreadsheet with a bunch of VBScript functions.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: I’ve personally moved almost entirely from Excel to Python models, where I can get believable simulations of things.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: The thing AI hasn’t come to yet, but I think it will within the next year—probably within 2026—and that will be very exciting: right now it can generate software, but soon it will generate STEP files and PCB layouts.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: When it comes for mechanical and electrical engineering, that’s a whole other thing we haven’t seen yet.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: On the hardware side, this is a boon for all these little gadget companies and part companies that write really bad software because they can’t make great software.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Now they’re going to be able to make good-enough software.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Or it may not even be software with a human front end—it might just be completely agentic, an agent accessing it, and you talk to it through voice to control hardware.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: This is one of the reasons China is big into open-source models.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: They’re going all in on it because they have hardware superiority.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: They have these very complex supply chains and component chains.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: They’re basically saying—“hey, if I can just generate software on demand, then I don’t have this disadvantage anymore against Silicon Valley.” That’s not the only reason they’re doing open source.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: They’re also behind, they’re distilling models, they’re catching up, they’re collaborating on resources.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: But the Chinese government has a history of funding efforts that help their entire ecosystem along, especially in network-effect businesses.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: They want to pool all their resources, catch up on AI, and use it to give their hardware stuff an advantage.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Ironically, they’re doing all the open-source stuff because OpenAI is not open.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Grok publishes models, but they’re a model or two behind.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Google has some local models, nothing really competitive.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Anthropic, to my knowledge—I don’t even know of any open-source models from them.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: So all the open-source heft is coming from China.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: It helps our hardware founders, but it helps their hardware founders and factories that much more.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: All the crappy little software that goes with all the random knickknacks and thingamajigs you buy off Amazon to tinker with on a lazy Saturday afternoon—that software’s getting a lot better very quickly.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Everyone’s had the wake-up call that without great frontier coding models, you don’t have self-improvement.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Imagine China as a whole not having the ability to produce frontier everything.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: It’s not just about producing software—in any piece of this hardware pipeline, like Blake was saying, you need to generate software.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: If you fall behind in your ability to generate software, you fall behind in your ability to generate everything.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: One thing I’m curious about: everyone loves to talk about Chinese models.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: This is an argument I had yesterday at dinner.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: One person at the table was claiming you’ll just use DeepSeek for 97% of things because it’s so cheap, and if you need more intelligence you’ll just run it over and over again—the same problem.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: You’ll only use OpenAI, Anthropic, etc. for the most advanced tasks.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: I was kind of like, “I don’t know.” I think intelligence is an unalloyed good.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: When these models make a mistake, you don’t know it.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And it’s always cheaper than a real person, and real-time.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: So you’ll just use the most intelligent model available.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Which isn’t great news, because it means you’ll end up creating a monopoly or oligopoly situation in AI.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: But I always want the most intelligent programmer.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Given the amount of leverage I’m going to pour into it—through capital and code and people and marketing—I want to make the right decision every time.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: When I have two models, one I know is a little smarter than the next, and they both give me answers, often I don’t actually know which is the correct answer.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: So if I know one model is a little smarter, I’m going to go with that answer, and eventually I’m going to stop asking the model I think is less intelligent.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Have you guys found a use for these so-called less intelligent models?
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: We have AI Gateway data—basically every application agent goes through it.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: There’s definitely usage of open models, but the top is heavily dominated by frontier intelligence.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: There’s a caveat: frontier intelligence at reasonable cost and performance slaps at scale.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Gemini—people don’t get really excited about Gemini, but they put out models that are super smart at the right performance-cost combination.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: For a lot of tasks other than coding, interestingly enough, they’re the best models.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: You can throw them at support tasks or browser automation.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: I’d always put a Gemini model there, and I’d look to Chinese models for those kinds of things.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: But any time I’m working to push the frontier, you need the best possible coding model.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Max, you’re pushing pretty hard into vertical integration and extreme urgency.
speaker: Nivi

claim: For many things, you can’t buy it, so you have to make it somehow.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: We obviously don’t do this on things like frontier models—I have an Anthropic subscription.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: We actually do use some of the Chinese models, to Naval’s point.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: We have a big internal fine-tune of 3.2 that I use for a bunch of things—we’re going to look into porting to 4 soon.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: But that’s on the personal side, not on the company side.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: Our preference would always be to buy something.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: If there’s a vendor that offers a service at a great price—for example, PCBs.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: You can buy them in unlimited quantity from Asia.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: But the closer our products get to being a single block of covalently bonded matter, the better they’ll be.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: Lower power, smaller, higher performance, longer lasting.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: In order to do that type of integration—to actually innovate beyond just piecing together things you can buy off the shelf, which is really very limiting—you have to learn to do it yourself.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: So we own a captive MEMS foundry on the East Coast.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: There was no other way to do the type of packaging and assembly we wanted to do.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: All of this is going to be affected heavily by AI over the next few years.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: Ironically, one of the biggest impacts we’ve seen of AI inside the company is in regulatory interactions.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: If we can generate documentation, or if we can ask—“we want to evolve this product, there are thousands of ISO standards that might apply, which ones do we have to comply with, trace this through”—that used to require a whole regulatory and quality team for several months.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: When I think about stuff like the surgical program or the MEMS fab—ultimately the software still needs hands.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: It’s going to be smarter than us, but if it can’t make things, those are real boundaries.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: We’ve instrumented our foundry as well as many other parts of the company in ways where, as these models get better, that should show up pretty immediately in things like the cell engineering we’re doing and the material science we’re developing.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: Our protein engineering group really uses deep learning a lot—I think we’re probably state of the art there.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: It means different things in different parts of the company.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: What Max was talking about with regulatory stuff makes me realize—it’s been a while since I generated a basic legal document using a lawyer.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: I stopped asking lawyers for NDAs, agreements for this, sign that, research this.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: There’s the old joke that law is like spaghetti code—very complicated code they try to put in English.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: It contradicts this code over here, has to fit into that code over here.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: For junior engineers and junior engineering—junior engineers basically got a promotion to senior engineer, and junior engineering got taken over by agents.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: The same way, in law, you can say “paralegals just got fired,” or you can say “paralegals just got promoted to senior lawyers, and now they can spend their time thinking about the law.”
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: It’s actually interesting to think about the parallels between how software engineering is evolving and lawyers.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: You never know exactly what lawyers put into these documents—you just trust them.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Can you do red lines?” What you’re valuing in the relationship with a lawyer is that they’re a trusted authority.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: They’re putting their reputation on the line.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: There’s a parallel with software engineering.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: The biggest problem today is this mountain of slop that ends up as a PR.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: There are all these memes on Twitter—“way back in the day we used to read every line of code of a PR.” Well, in my world—infrastructure—I want engineers to be able to say “I understand” every line of that PR.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: That doesn’t necessarily mean you’ve read every line.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: It means you can say “I understand the consequences of this PR.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim:
I’m signing off on understanding the consequences.” Or, “I wrote the test harness, the simulations, the proofs, the type-checkers—even without reading this, I have confidence I can sign off that it’ll be safe in production.”

There’s a world in which we embrace that everything is going to be spaghetti code we don’t fully understand, but we write the evaluators that give us confidence, and we rely on people—the infrastructure production engineers—to say, “Okay, I’m fine sending this into prod.” Someone is going to get paged if your systems go down.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Another thing people are underestimating: creating software is really easy, zero to one.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: And are you still motivated to invest all those tokens in maintaining it in prod?
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: That’s how we train these models—with good verification data—and now we need human verifiers.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: A lot of the old function of people, lawyers, engineers, operations people, moves to verifying the stack and saying, “Yeah, this is roughly correct, I’ll roughly stand behind it, I’ll support you if it goes wrong.”
speaker: Naval Ravikant
